text: A gastric bypass is an operation where we are going to not only decrease the capacity of the stomach to allow eating smaller amounts in a comfortable environment, we're also going to bypass a section of the intestinal tract to allow for some malabsorption so that the weight loss comes quicker and easier over time. The first part of the operation involves identifying the appropriate anatomy and evaluating the small bowel. We then create the small bowel limb that will come up to the excluded portion of the stomach. We create what we call a Roux limb by transecting the small bowel and creating enough space for that limb to come up to the excluded stomach.

The surgeon reconnects the transected small bowel by aligning two loops and creating an anastomosis using a stapler device, followed by hand-sewn sutures to close the remaining connection. The defect between the loops is closed to prevent an internal hernia, completing the small bowel portion of the gastric bypass procedure.

The surgeon brings the small bowel limb over the colon to the stomach, preparing it for connection to the excluded portion of the stomach. A small pouch is created from the stomach using a stapling device, forming a 15 to 20 cc pouch that restricts food intake. The pouch is then connected to the small bowel through a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy, with small holes made in both the stomach and small intestine to allow food passage. The holes are approximately one to one and a half centimeters in size, ensuring controlled food passage.

The surgeon places two separate layers of sutures to bring the stomach together and reinforce the staple line, minimizing the risk of leakage. The sutures are tied off after the first layer, and a second layer is added under direct vision with hand-sewn suturing. An orogastric tube is inserted into the pouch, which is distended with dyed fluid to test for leaks. After confirming no leakage, biologic glue is applied to reinforce the area and prevent post-operative bleeding. A drainage catheter is positioned around the anastomosis site, completing the procedure.
